aliases: Assorted Seafood Stew with Tomato Garlic Sauce

english: Assorted Seafood Stew with Tomato Garlic Sauce

food_subgroup: Seafood stew with tomato-based sauce and bread

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Zuppa di Mare; 'seafood stew' (FOODON_03315763) is the closest parent category covering mixed seafood in a tomato-based broth.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Zuppa di Mare; A0CKP (Fish and seafood based dishes) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for Zuppa di Mare or Italian seafood stew as a prepared dish.

main_ingredients: mixed seafood (prawns, mussels, clams, squid, fish fillet)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Zuppa di Mare is approximately 450 g, comprising a generous bowl of mixed seafood stew in tomato-garlic broth accompanied by one or two slices of rustic bread.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Fish and Seafood Stew, tomato-based, with mixed seafood; supplemented with USDA SR Legacy entries for individual seafood components and bread
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry exists for Zuppa di Mare as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-component method using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for: mixed seafood (prawns, mussels, clams, squid, fish fillet) at approximately 55% by weight, tomato-garlic sauce at approximately 30% by weight, and rustic bread at approximately 15% by weight. Energy and macros are cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central entry #174202 (Seafood stew, home-prepared) and #171506 (Cioppino, seafood stew). Values represent the composite dish per 100 g edible portion.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J; analogous estimate based on mixed seafood stew with tomato sauce and bread
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Zuppa di Mare. GI estimated at 45 based on the composite dish profile: the seafood and tomato-sauce components contribute negligible glycemic load (GI ~15–25 for tomato-based sauces per University of Sydney GI Database), while the rustic bread component (approximately 15% by weight, GI ~55 for sourdough/ciabatta per University of Sydney GI Database entry for sourdough bread) moderates the overall GI upward. Weighted composite GI estimated at 45, classified as low (≤55). Glycemic load per 450 g serving calculated as (45 × 37.8 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 17.0 g GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 420 mg per 100 g; a full 450 g serving delivers approximately 1890 mg sodium, approaching or exceeding the 2000 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should consume Zuppa di Mare in reduced portions and request low-sodium preparation, limiting or omitting added salt and using fresh tomatoes rather than canned, to keep the per-serving sodium contribution below 600 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Estimated glycemic index of 45 (low) and moderate carbohydrate content of 8.4 g per 100 g; however, the bread component raises the glycemic load of a full serving to approximately 17 g GL, which requires portion awareness.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes can include Zuppa di Mare as part of a balanced meal given its low GI and high protein content from seafood, but should limit or omit the accompanying bread to reduce the glycemic load per serving to below 10 g GL.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Rich in omega-3 fatty acids from mixed seafood (mussels, clams, prawns, fish fillet) and low in saturated fat (0.6 g per 100 g); olive oil provides cardioprotective monounsaturated fats.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Zuppa di Mare is generally favourable for cardiovascular health due to its high omega-3 seafood content and low saturated fat profile; however, individuals with cardiovascular disease should monitor sodium intake and request reduced-salt preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Zuppa di Mare (Italian: 'soup of the sea') is a classic Italian seafood stew originating from the coastal regions of Italy, particularly popular in Liguria, Campania, and Sicily. The dish consists of an assortment of fresh seafood — typically prawns, mussels, clams, squid, and white fish fillet — simmered together in a rich tomato-garlic sauce made with olive oil, white wine, fresh parsley, basil, and chilli flakes. It is traditionally served with thick slices of rustic bread such as ciabatta or sourdough, which are used to soak up the flavourful broth. The texture of the dish is hearty and brothy, with tender seafood pieces in a slightly acidic, aromatic tomato base. The flavour profile is savoury, garlicky, mildly spicy, and deeply umami from the shellfish. Nutritionally, Zuppa di Mare is a high-protein, moderate-carbohydrate dish with approximately 9.2 g protein per 100 g, low saturated fat (0.6 g per 100 g), and a meaningful contribution of vitamin B12, iron, selenium, and omega-3 fatty acids from the mixed seafood. The estimated glycemic index is 45 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management when the bread portion is controlled. The sodium content is notable at approximately 420 mg per 100 g, requiring attention for hypertensive individuals. In Singapore, Zuppa di Mare is served at Italian restaurants such as Ricciotti at The Riverwalk and is positioned as a premium seafood main course.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Zuppa di Mare as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-component method using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for individual seafood, tomato sauce, and bread components, cross-checked against USDA entries for analogous seafood stews (Cioppino, home-prepared seafood stew). GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for component foods. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from input value 'Grains and staples' to 'Fish and seafood', which accurately reflects the primary composition of Zuppa di Mare as a seafood-dominant dish. 2. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-component method (seafood ~55%, tomato-garlic sauce ~30%, rustic bread ~15%) using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data; no single approved-source entry exists for this composite Italian restaurant dish. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero for this dish as it contains no hydrogenated fats; olive oil and seafood are the primary fat sources. 4. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous composite calculation from University of Sydney GI Database component values; no direct GI measurement for Zuppa di Mare exists in the literature. 5. Glycemic load per 450 g serving = (45 × 37.8 g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 17.0 g GL. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all estimated from USDA component data and should be treated as approximate. 7. Halal status is unconfirmed for the Ricciotti restaurant preparation; the dish may contain wine (white wine is a traditional ingredient) which would render it non-halal. 8. This is an Italian cuisine dish; multilingual naming rules for Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, and Vietnamese cuisines do not apply. Local names in Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are not required and are left null. The romanized Italian name 'Zuppa di Mare' serves as the canonical identifier across all languages.
